region_of_origin: Eastern China — Jiangnan region (Shanghai, Zhejiang, Jiangsu)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for drunken shrimp (alcohol-marinated live freshwater shrimp). Mapped to the closest parent class 'shrimp food product' (FOODON_03301710) as the most defensible FoodOn anchor. Match type is closest_parent as the specific preparation is not represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CJW (Shrimps and prawns) used as the closest parent category. No specific FoodEx2 code exists for alcohol-marinated raw freshwater shrimp preparation. Match type is closest_parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for drunken shrimp as a specific food concept.

main_ingredients: Baijiu (high-proof Chinese liquor) or aged Shaoxing wine

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Drunken Shrimp is approximately 100 g, representing a small side-dish portion of alcohol-marinated freshwater shrimp served at a shared Chinese meal.

nutrient_source.title: Shrimp, raw — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are based on raw shrimp from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175180) as the closest approved-source equivalent. Sodium is elevated to reflect the soy sauce marinade contribution (estimated ~450 mg/100 g for the finished dish). Minor adjustments for carbohydrate and sugar reflect marinade ingredients (Shaoxing wine, soy sauce). All other macros and micronutrients are anchored to the USDA raw shrimp profile. The input record's values are consistent with this adaptation and have been retained where they align.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — Atkinson et al. (2008); analogous estimate for high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal, American Diabetes Association)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Drunken Shrimp. GI is estimated at 15 (low) by analogy with other high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood preparations documented in Atkinson et al. (2008). Carbohydrate content is 2.5 g/100 g; glycemic load per 100 g serving = (15 × 2.5) / 100 = 0.4, classified as very low. The alcohol and vinegar components of the marinade may further moderate glycemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium from soy sauce marinade (~450 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size or request a reduced-soy-sauce preparation, as the marinade contributes approximately 450 mg sodium per 100 g, representing roughly 20% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g) and high protein (14.5 g/100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Drunken Shrimp is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes due to its very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g), high protein, and estimated low glycemic index of 15, resulting in a glycemic load of only 0.4 per serving; however, alcohol intake from the marinade should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Shrimp cholesterol content (~152 mg/100 g) and elevated sodium from marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While shrimp is low in saturated fat (0.2 g/100 g) and provides cardioprotective selenium and omega-3 fatty acids, individuals with cardiovascular disease should be mindful of the elevated sodium (~450 mg/100 g) and moderate dietary cholesterol (~152 mg/100 g), and should limit consumption of the alcohol-heavy marinade.

food_safety.key_factor: Raw freshwater shrimp carries risk of Vibrio, Salmonella, and parasitic infection
food_safety.guidance: Pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals, and those with liver disease should avoid the traditional raw preparation of Drunken Shrimp due to high risk of bacterial (Vibrio, Salmonella) and parasitic infection; the briefly blanched modern variant is significantly safer.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Drunken Shrimp (醉虾, Zuì xiā) is a celebrated and controversial delicacy from the Jiangnan region of Eastern China, encompassing Shanghai, Zhejiang, and Jiangsu provinces. The dish is defined by its extraordinary preparation: small, live freshwater shrimp are submerged in a potent marinade of high-proof Baijiu (Chinese grain liquor) or aged Shaoxing rice wine, combined with ginger, garlic, scallions, and soy sauce. The alcohol rapidly intoxicates and effectively 'cures' the shrimp without heat, yielding a raw, translucent flesh that is exceptionally sweet, tender, and delicate, infused with the deep, complex aromatics of the liquor. The dish is served chilled or at room temperature and is prized as a refined appetizer or side dish at banquets and social dining occasions. Nutritionally, Drunken Shrimp is high in lean protein (14.5 g/100 g), very low in fat (0.8 g/100 g) and carbohydrates (2.5 g/100 g), and provides meaningful amounts of selenium (25.5 mcg), vitamin B12 (1.2 mcg), phosphorus (160 mg), and zinc (1.2 mg). Sodium is elevated (~450 mg/100 g) due to the soy sauce marinade. The glycemic index is estimated at 15 (low), making it suitable for blood sugar management. Due to significant food safety concerns associated with raw freshwater seafood — including risk of Vibrio, Salmonella, and parasitic infection — modern restaurant versions often briefly blanch the shrimp before marinating. The dish is not suitable for pregnant women, immunocompromised individuals, or those with liver disease in its traditional raw form.
common_user_queries: Is drunken shrimp safe to eat?
Is drunken shrimp good for diabetics?
What alcohol is used in drunken shrimp?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw shrimp data (FDC ID 175180), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. No exact entry for alcohol-marinated drunken shrimp exists in any approved primary source. Sodium is estimated to reflect marinade contribution. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed literature. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are anchored to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw shrimp (FDC ID 175180) with sodium adjusted upward to ~450 mg/100 g to reflect soy sauce marinade contribution. Minor carbohydrate and sugar values reflect Shaoxing wine and soy sauce. 2. water_g is set to -1: detailed moisture data for the finished alcohol-marinated preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. 3. Dietary fibre is 0.0 g — confirmed as naturally zero for shrimp flesh; this is consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for raw shrimp. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for raw shrimp per USDA FoodData Central. 5. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate based on high-protein, very-low-carbohydrate seafood in Atkinson et al. (2008); no direct GI measurement for drunken shrimp exists in any approved GI source. 6. Cholesterol value of 152 mg/100 g is sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw shrimp. 7. Saturated fat (0.2 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.1 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.3 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy raw shrimp fatty acid profile. 8. The duplicate portion entry in the input record has been deduplicated to a single 100 g serving. 9. Food safety advisory: traditional raw preparation carries significant risk of Vibrio, Salmonella, and parasitic infection; not suitable for pregnant, immunocompromised, or liver-disease patients. 10. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2, as 'broader' is not an allowed value; closest_parent accurately reflects that the mapped codes represent parent-level categories rather than exact matches for this specific preparation.
